dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The plan is factually accurate. The skill correctly identified that pl_002 is completed and created a new pl_003 in add-new mode. All plan items target record types appropriate to the research question and supported by tool responses. Church collection 1401638, volumes 007936749-753, and Orphans' Court information all verified through MCP calls. Rationales for each item are specific and grounded in the scenario: Thomas Flynn parentage requires direct evidence from church marriage records, siblings' baptisms, or probate/Orphans' Court records. No fabricated claims; all supported by sources.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The plan comprehensively addresses the user's request to focus on church and probate records next. It includes: church records (indexed and browse-only films), Orphans' Court (distinct from the prior Register of Wills search), unindexed probate browse films, naturalization records for confirming identity and Irish origin, Patrick's marriage record (corroboration of father naming), and Ancestry probate as paid backup. All seven items are complete with sequencing, jurisdiction, date ranges, record types, and detailed rationales. The plan follows a clear free-to-paid, indexed-to-unindexed sequence.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All MCP tool calls passed arguments matching the fixture's expected_args. place_search received 'Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania' (satisfied ~Schuylkill). collections_search and volume_search both passed standardPlace='Schuylkill, Pennsylvania, United States' (matched ~Schuylkill). wiki_search queries matched ~Schuylkill and ~probate predicates. external_links_search satisfied ~Schuylkill. All calls succeeded with matched.kind='predicate' or 'live'; no fixture_not_found errors. research_append correctly formatted ops array with all required fields for plan and item appends. No critical argument errors; all identifiers and parameters are correct.

dimensions.name: Record type selection
dimensions.rationale: Every plan item's record_type directly matches the research question's information need. Church records (pli_007, pli_008) target marriage and baptism evidence to identify the mother and confirm both parents. Probate/Orphans' Court (pli_009, pli_010) target direct parentage evidence when a parent died with minor children. Naturalization (pli_011) confirms identity and Irish origin. Marriage record (pli_012) provides contemporaneous corroboration of paternity. Each rationale explains specifically what information the record type provides and why it advances the Thomas Flynn parentage question under BCG standards. No generic or misaligned record types.

dimensions.name: Sequencing logic
dimensions.rationale: The plan follows defensible free/indexed-before-paid/unindexed logic. Items 1–2 are church records (free, indexed first then browse). Items 3–4 are Orphans' Court and probate (free, browse-only). Item 5 is naturalization (free). Item 6 is marriage (free). Item 7 is Ancestry (paid). Two explicit fallback chains are documented: pli_008 fallback_for pli_007 (church indexed→browse), and pli_010 fallback_for pli_009 (Orphans' Court→unindexed probate film). Each item's rationale explains why it appears in its sequence position. This reflects a coherent research strategy: exhaust free indexed sources, then unindexed FamilySearch films, then paid alternatives.

dimensions.name: Jurisdiction accuracy
dimensions.rationale: All jurisdictions are historically accurate for the target period (1840–1910). Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania existed and maintained church, probate, and naturalization records throughout this period. The plan correctly identifies that Schuylkill County was formed in 1811 (confirmed by place_search result) and church records date from 1842–1895 within the county. No anachronistic jurisdictions or pre-statehood errors. Date ranges on each item (1840–1875 for church, 1851–1930 for probate, 1849–1915 for naturalization) reflect when records were actually kept and digitized in the county.

dimensions.name: Plan mode and lifecycle
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly applied add-new mode. pl_002 was completed (all items done) and the question remains in_progress with proof_summary at probable. Creating pl_003 as a new active plan is the correct action; the skill did not attempt to edit or reuse pl_002. The new plan was written with status='active' and all items have status='planned'. No duplicate active plans are present; the prior completed plan (pl_002) remains in the project state as an audit trail. The lifecycle is sound: one completed plan, one new active plan, no conflicting statuses.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All outputs are factually accurate and properly grounded. The plan correctly identifies that pl_002 is completed and q_001 remains in_progress at 'Probable' tier, triggering add-new mode. All tool calls returned valid data (place standardization, collections matching Schuylkill County 1845-1910, parish records including the 1842-1895 unindexed group 007936751, and naturalization/land/probate volumes). The plan items cite real FamilySearch collections with accurate record counts, date ranges, and percentages indexed. No fabricated material; no unsupported claims.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed all requirements: created a new plan (pl_003) with 10 items after the prior completed plan; included multiple FAN-pivot items (siblings' death certificates, Thomas Flynn's naturalization, Patrick's census neighbors, Patrick's marriage); explained why each record type advances the parentage question; sequenced items logically (1880 census first to identify siblings, church records second for mother's name, marriage records fourth, siblings' death certificates sixth as the core FAN payoff); identified fallbacks (Step 2 → Step 3 for church records); and persisted all changes to research.json with valid schema.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All 8 MCP calls passed args matching expected values: place_search used 'Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania' (satisfies ~Schuylkill); wiki_place_page correctly targeted Pennsylvania research_tips and online_records sections; collections_search and external_links_search both used standardPlace matching ~Schuylkill; volume_search queried Schuylkill with matching date range; wiki_search queries paraphrased but matched semantic intent (~Pennsylvania, ~census records). The research_append call correctly batched 11 operations (1 plan append + 10 plan_items appends) with valid IDs (pl_003, pli_007–pli_016), all parameters properly structured.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Record type selection
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Every plan item's record_type is justified by information need. Census (1880, 1870, 1900) targets relationship-to-head statements and family clustering. Church (Steps 2, 3, 4) targets baptismal and marriage records naming both parents—the only source type supplying the unknown mother's name. Vital records (Step 6) targets Pennsylvania death certificates post-1906 for siblings naming parents. Other/naturalization (Step 9) targets county court records naming wife and children. Land (Step 10) targets deeds showing joint grants by Thomas and spouse, supplying mother's name. Each rationale explicitly explains what information the type provides (e.g., Step 2: 'baptismal record…names both father and mother…the only source type that can supply the mother's identity').

aggregated_dimensions.name: Sequencing logic
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Sequence reflects free/indexed before paid/unindexed and broad before narrow. Items 1–3 are indexed or free (1880 census, indexed church collection 1401638, then fallback unindexed volume 007936751). Items 5, 7 (censuses), 6 (death certs) follow. Items 8–10 (marriage, naturalization, land) close the sequence. Fallback_for explicitly chains Step 2 → Step 3 (pli_008 → pli_009). Each rationale explains placement: Step 1 comes first because it identifies siblings needed for Step 6; Steps 2–4 come early because church records are most likely to supply the mother's name; Step 6 depends on sibling names from Steps 1–5; Steps 7–10 fill gaps and provide corroboration. The sequence is logically ordered and fallback dependencies are explicit.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Jurisdiction accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All jurisdictions are historically accurate for 1840–1910 Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania. County court naturalization authority pre-1906 is correctly stated. output.text_response:
Let me start by reading the project state to understand the question, what's been searched, and what the subject's context is.Good — both plans for `q_001` are `completed` and the question is still `in_progress` (proof only at "Probable"). This is **add-new mode**: I need to create `pl_003` with a FAN-focused plan. Let me read the references and run the locality survey in parallel.Good — `standardPlace` is "Schuylkill, Pennsylvania, United States". Now let me survey collections, external links, and volumes all at once.Now let me check volumes for church/land/naturalization records that may only be browse-only films.I now have a complete picture. Here's the project state going into the new plan:

- **q_001** (parentage of Patrick Flynn) is `in_progress`; proof is at "Probable" — not yet proved
- **pl_001** (q_002 census) and **pl_002** (q_001 direct sources) are both `completed`
- Searched so far for q_001: 1860 census, 1908 death cert, Schuylkill probate (negative)
- **Gaps confirmed**: 1870/1880/1900 censuses never searched; mother's name unknown; no church, marriage, naturalization, land, or sibling records searched
- Next plan ID = **pl_003**; next item IDs = **pli_007** through **pli_016**

**FAN plan design:** 10 items targeting Thomas Flynn's household records, the Flynn sibling cluster, church baptisms (which name both parents), Thomas Flynn's own marriage/naturalization/land records, and Patrick's later censuses and marriage. Writing now in one batched call.The tool requires top-level `section` and `op` even when using the `ops` array. Retrying with those fields included:**pl_003 written successfully** — 10 items, all `planned`, no validation warnings. Here is the plan:

---

## Plan pl_003 — FAN Approach for Parentage of Patrick Flynn (q_001)

**Why a new plan?** Two prior plans (pl_001, pl_002) are completed. Direct sources — 1860 census, 1908 death certificate, Schuylkill probate — establish a "Probable" conclusion but not a proved one. Patrick's mother is entirely unknown. The 1870, 1880, and 1900 censuses were never searched. A FAN pivot is warranted.

---

### Step 1 — **pli_007 | 1880 Census, Schuylkill County** | FamilySearch
The 1880 federal census is the first to record every household member's *explicit relationship* to the head. If Thomas Flynn is alive, a listing of Patrick as his son is **primary-source direct evidence of paternity** — the strongest remaining census source by far. If Thomas has died, his widow's or a sibling's household anchors the family cluster and supplies the sibling names needed for Step 6. Search both Thomas and Patrick Flynn simultaneously.

---

### Step 2 — **pli_008 | Church Baptisms, Schuylkill County, 1840–1870** | FamilySearch (collection 1401638)
A Catholic baptismal record for Patrick or any sibling names *both* father and mother (including mother's maiden name) — the most direct parentage evidence possible and the only source type that can supply the mother's identity. The Schuylkill County Irish Catholic mining community was large and well-documented in parish registers from the 1840s.

### Step 3 — **pli_009 | Browse-only Church Records, 1842–1895** | FamilySearch (image group 007936751) ← *fallback for Step 2*
Many Catholic sacramental registers were microfilmed but never indexed. This 187-image unindexed group requires browsing; it pre-dates the 1868 Philadelphia diocesan split and covers precisely the period when Patrick and his siblings would have been baptized.

---

### Step 4 — **pli_010 | Thomas Flynn's Marriage Record, 1840–1860** | FamilySearch (church records collection 1401638 + browse-only marriage image group 007936753)
The **dedicated "candidate parents' marriage to each other" item**. Thomas Flynn's marriage to Patrick's mother names the bride — the only way to identify the mother — and its date, if before ~1845, directly confirms Thomas had a wife when Patrick was born. Both the indexed church collection and the browse-only county marriage image group must be checked.

---

### Step 5 — **pli_011 | 1870 Census, Schuylkill County** | FamilySearch
Patrick (~25) may still be in Thomas's household or living adjacent to it. Thomas Flynn's 1870 household identifies which siblings remained at home — their names are required for Step 6. Even if the 1870 census shows Patrick independently, proximity to the Thomas Flynn cluster is corroborating indirect evidence.

---

### Step 6 — **pli_012 | Siblings' Pennsylvania Death Certificates, 1906–1940** | FamilySearch (collection 2255317)
**The core FAN step.** Any sibling of Patrick who died in Pennsylvania after 1906 would have a death certificate naming *both* parents. The siblings identified in Steps 1 and 5 are the targets here. A sibling's death certificate naming Thomas Flynn as father *and supplying the mother's maiden name* would advance the conclusion from Probable to **Proved**.

---

### Step 7 — **pli_013 | 1900 Census, Schuylkill County (Patrick Flynn)** | FamilySearch
Captures Patrick ~8 years before his death; fills the 1860–1908 timeline gap. Records years married and children born/living. Patrick's 1900 neighbors (FAN cluster) may include Flynn relatives with their own documentary links to Thomas Flynn.

---

### Step 8 — **pli_014 | Patrick's Marriage Record, 1865–1895** | FamilySearch (collection 1921317 + image group 007936753)
Patrick's marriage record may name his parents. Even if not, confirming his wife's identity opens a path to her obituary or death certificate, which may name Thomas Flynn as her father-in-law.

---

### Step 9 — **pli_015 | Thomas Flynn's Naturalization, 1849–1890** | FamilySearch (browse-only image group 007936750) + Ancestry fallback
Pre-1906 Irish immigrant naturalization through Schuylkill County court. Declarations of intent sometimes name the applicant's wife and children; the petition fixes the county of origin in Ireland, potentially opening parish record searches there.

---

### Step 10 — **pli_016 | Land / Deed Records, Schuylkill County, 1845–1900** | FamilySearch (image group 007936752, 22% indexed)
If Thomas conveyed property to his children, the deed names the co-grantees (potentially Patrick). Joint conveyances with his wife supply the mother's name. Start with the 22% indexed portion; browse the remainder if Thomas Flynn is not found.

---

### Fallback relationship
| Primary | Fallback |
|---|---|
| Step 2 — indexed church records (pli_008) | Step 3 — browse-only church records (pli_009) |

---

### Scope and next step
**10 searches** across census, church, vital, naturalization, and land record types — FamilySearch for all items, with Ancestry noted as a cross-check for Steps 6 and 9.

**The payoff sequence:** Steps 1 → 2/3 → 4 → 6 are the highest-value items; any one of them could supply the missing mother's name and move the conclusion to Proved. Would you like to start executing — beginning with the 1880 census (Step 1)?

items.rationale: The 1880 census is the first federal enumeration to record each household member's explicit relationship to the head of household — making it the most powerful remaining census source for this parentage question. If Thomas Flynn (the candidate father) is alive in 1880 with Patrick listed as his son, that entry is primary-source direct evidence of paternity, stronger than anything found so far. Even if Thomas has died by 1880, his widow's or a sibling's 1880 household would anchor the family cluster and identify other children (Patrick's siblings) whose names are needed for the sibling death-certificate step. Patrick Flynn should also be searched in this same census — at age ~35 he is likely in his own household, but neighboring the Thomas Flynn family or a known sibling would reinforce the identification and reveal FAN associates.

A Catholic baptismal record for Patrick Flynn or any of his siblings is the highest-quality parentage evidence available — it would name both father and mother (including mother's maiden name), which no source found so far has supplied. Patrick's mother is entirely unknown from any record examined. Schuylkill County's anthracite coal workforce in the 1840s-1850s was heavily Irish Catholic; St. Patrick's Church in Pottsville and other early parishes kept sacramental registers from this period. FamilySearch's indexed 'Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708-1985' (collection 1401638, ~1.2 million records) covers multiple denominations and should be searched for all Flynn baptisms in the county.

Fallback for the indexed church records search (step 2): many Catholic sacramental registers were microfilmed but never indexed and would not surface in a keyword search of collection 1401638. FamilySearch browse-only image group 007936751 (187 images, 1842-1895, 0% indexed) holds unindexed Schuylkill County church records requiring page-by-page browsing. This is especially important for Catholic parishes — the Diocese of Philadelphia originally covered this area and split into the Harrisburg and Scranton dioceses in 1868; early Catholic registers (pre-1868) may appear in this image group rather than in later diocesan archives.

This is the dedicated 'candidate parents' marriage to each other' item. Thomas Flynn's marriage to Patrick's mother is the single most important unlocated record: it names the bride — Patrick's mother — whose name is entirely unknown from any source found so far. The marriage date, if before ~1845 (Patrick's estimated birth year), directly confirms Thomas had a wife of record when Patrick was born. Both the indexed church records collection (1401638) and the browse-only marriage records image group (007936753, 1870-1910, 0% indexed) should be checked; a marriage before civil registration would appear in Catholic or Protestant church registers. If the marriage date post-dates 1870, the civil records image group becomes the primary target.

In 1870 Patrick (~25) may still be in Thomas Flynn's household or living nearby. Locating Thomas Flynn's household in 1870 identifies which siblings were still resident — their names are needed for the death-certificate search in step 6. Locating Patrick separately in 1870, if he has left the parental home, places him geographically and may reveal nearby Flynn relatives (FAN associates). Like the 1850 and 1860 enumerations, the 1870 census does not state relationships, but co-residence or proximity corroborates the family unit and confirms Thomas Flynn's continued presence in the county.

This is the core FAN step: any sibling of Patrick Flynn who died in Pennsylvania after 1906 (when mandatory state death registration began) would have a death certificate naming both parents — father Thomas Flynn and, critically, the mother whose name remains entirely unknown. The 1850 and 1860 census households (and the 1880 household from step 1) should supply the siblings' given names. Searching FamilySearch 'Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906-1968' (collection 2255317) for those individuals — or broadly for Flynn deaths in Schuylkill County listing Thomas Flynn as father — could supply the mother's maiden name and advance the conclusion from 'Probable' to 'Proved'. Ancestry holds a parallel indexed version as a cross-check.

Patrick Flynn is known to have died 12 March 1908. The 1900 census captures him ~8 years earlier (age ~55) in his established Schuylkill County household. The 1900 enumeration records years married, number of children born, and children living — data that helps reconstruct his immediate family. Critically, Patrick's neighbors in the 1900 ward (the FAN cluster) may include Flynn relatives or associates with documentary links to Thomas Flynn. This census also fills a documented 1860-1908 gap in the timeline and may place Patrick adjacent to surviving Thomas Flynn household members.

Patrick Flynn's own marriage record may name his parents. FamilySearch 'Pennsylvania, County Marriages, 1885-1950' (collection 1921317) covers the likely range; the browse-only marriage image group (007936753, 1870-1910, 0% indexed) extends coverage to an earlier marriage. Even if the record does not name parents, confirming Patrick's wife's full name and marriage date fixes his identity and may lead to her obituary or death certificate, which could name her father-in-law Thomas Flynn as a surviving relative at the time of her death.

As an Irish immigrant enumerated in Schuylkill County from at least 1850, Thomas Flynn would have naturalized through a county court (Quarter Sessions or Common Pleas — pre-1906 naturalizations occurred at any court of record in Pennsylvania, not only federal courts). Declarations of intent sometimes name the applicant's wife and children; petitions for citizenship confirm country of origin and arrival date, which could open Irish civil registration or Catholic parish record searches for the Flynn family in Ireland. FamilySearch browse-only volume 007936750 (298 images, 1849-1915, 0% indexed) holds Schuylkill County naturalization records and requires browsing. Ancestry's 'Pennsylvania, U.S., Naturalization Records, 1794-1931' is a partially indexed fallback.

Deed books at the Schuylkill County Recorder of Deeds may name Thomas Flynn as grantor or grantee. If Thomas conveyed property to his children, the deed would name the co-grantees — potentially confirming Patrick as a son. If Thomas and his wife jointly conveyed property, the wife's signature and name appear, supplying the mother's name. FamilySearch browse-only volume 007936752 (543 images, 1815-1900, 22% indexed) holds county deed records; a targeted index search for 'Thomas Flynn' should precede browsing the unindexed portion. The Pennsylvania State Archives in Harrisburg holds additional deed originals not fully digitized.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed all requirements: created a new plan (pl_003) with 10 items after the prior completed plan; included multiple FAN-pivot items (siblings' death certificates, Thomas Flynn's naturalization, Patrick's census neighbors, Patrick's marriage); explained why each record type advances the parentage question; sequenced items logically (1880 census first to identify siblings, church records second for mother's name, marriage records fourth, siblings' death certificates sixth as the core FAN payoff); identified fallbacks (Step 2 → Step 3 for church records); and persisted all changes to research.json with valid schema.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All 8 MCP calls passed args matching expected values: place_search used 'Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania' (satisfies ~Schuylkill); wiki_place_page correctly targeted Pennsylvania research_tips and online_records sections; collections_search and external_links_search both used standardPlace matching ~Schuylkill; volume_search queried Schuylkill with matching date range; wiki_search queries paraphrased but matched semantic intent (~Pennsylvania, ~census records). The research_append call correctly batched 11 operations (1 plan append + 10 plan_items appends) with valid IDs (pl_003, pli_007–pli_016), all parameters properly structured.

dimensions.name: Record type selection
dimensions.rationale: Every plan item's record_type is justified by information need. Census (1880, 1870, 1900) targets relationship-to-head statements and family clustering. Church (Steps 2, 3, 4) targets baptismal and marriage records naming both parents—the only source type supplying the unknown mother's name. Vital records (Step 6) targets Pennsylvania death certificates post-1906 for siblings naming parents. Other/naturalization (Step 9) targets county court records naming wife and children. Land (Step 10) targets deeds showing joint grants by Thomas and spouse, supplying mother's name. Each rationale explicitly explains what information the type provides (e.g., Step 2: 'baptismal record…names both father and mother…the only source type that can supply the mother's identity').

dimensions.name: Sequencing logic
dimensions.rationale: Sequence reflects free/indexed before paid/unindexed and broad before narrow. Items 1–3 are indexed or free (1880 census, indexed church collection 1401638, then fallback unindexed volume 007936751). Items 5, 7 (censuses), 6 (death certs) follow. Items 8–10 (marriage, naturalization, land) close the sequence. Fallback_for explicitly chains Step 2 → Step 3 (pli_008 → pli_009). Each rationale explains placement: Step 1 comes first because it identifies siblings needed for Step 6; Steps 2–4 come early because church records are most likely to supply the mother's name; Step 6 depends on sibling names from Steps 1–5; Steps 7–10 fill gaps and provide corroboration. The sequence is logically ordered and fallback dependencies are explicit.

dimensions.name: Jurisdiction accuracy
dimensions.rationale: All jurisdictions are historically accurate for 1840–1910 Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania. The plan correctly cites the Diocese of Philadelphia split into Harrisburg and Scranton dioceses in 1868 (affecting which Catholic register image groups to search). County court naturalization authority pre-1906 is correctly stated. Census, vital records (post-1906), church records, and land records are all correctly attributed to Schuylkill County courts and parish authorities as appropriate for the time period. No anachronisms; no recommendation to search non-existent jurisdictions. The standardPlace 'Schuylkill, Pennsylvania, United States' (confirmed by mcp__genealogy__place_search) is correct and consistent throughout.

## Research Plan: Post-1860 Federal Censuses for Patrick & Thomas Flynn
**Question q_001** — Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, d. 1908)?
**Plan pl_003** — 8 items across three census years

---

### Why this plan now

The previous plan (pl_002) is complete: 1860 census, Patrick's death certificate, and Schuylkill County probate have all been searched. The proof summary already flags **1870, 1880, and 1900** as the primary remaining gap keeping the parentage conclusion at *Probable* rather than *Proved*. The 1890 census is unavailable — destroyed by fire.

---

### Items in execution order

**Step 1 — pli_007 · 1870 Census · FamilySearch**
Patrick's first adult appearance (~age 25). Schuylkill County is the anchoring jurisdiction — he was there in 1860 and died there in 1908. The search establishes whether he stayed in the county and whether Thomas (~52) is still co-resident. The 1870 census does not yet have a relationship column, so co-residence is still indirect evidence, but a third consecutive co-enumeration meaningfully strengthens the hypothesis.

**Step 2 — pli_008 · 1870 Census · Ancestry** *(fallback for Step 1)*
If FamilySearch misses Patrick due to transcription error or a spelling variant (Flynn / Flinn / Flyn), Ancestry's independent 1870 index (NARA M593) is the safety net.

**Step 3 — pli_009 · 1870 Census · FamilySearch — Thomas Flynn**
A direct, independent search for Thomas Flynn regardless of what Step 1 finds. By 1870, Patrick may have moved out; Thomas may head his own household. This establishes Thomas's survival and whereabouts and may reveal siblings in his household — all useful context heading into 1880.

**Step 4 — pli_010 · 1880 Census · FamilySearch** ⭐ *Highest-value item*
**The 1880 census is the first federal enumeration to record explicit family relationships.** If Thomas (~62) appears in Patrick's household labeled *"father,"* or Patrick (~35) appears in Thomas's household labeled *"son,"* that is **direct evidence of parentage** — something the 1850 and 1860 censuses could not provide. A single positive result here could advance the conclusion from *Probable* to *Proved*.

**Step 5 — pli_011 · 1880 Census · Ancestry** *(fallback for Step 4)*
Given the exceptional importance of the 1880 relationship column, Ancestry's independent 1880 index (NARA T9) is the fallback if FamilySearch is negative or ambiguous.

**Step 6 — pli_012 · 1880 Census · FamilySearch — Thomas Flynn**
Direct 1880 search for Thomas (~62). Even if he and Patrick are in separate households, the relationship column on Thomas's entry labels everyone in his household explicitly — a *daughter-in-law* or *grandchildren* would also confirm the family. Absence from 1880 is itself evidence: it would point toward a pre-1880 death and refocus research on his death record and estate.

**Step 7 — pli_013 · 1900 Census · FamilySearch**
Patrick (~55) as an established householder. If Thomas is still living (~82), he could appear as a dependent parent — the 1900 census also records family relationships. Even without Thomas, this search completes the adult census chain from 1870 to death (1908), corroborates identity continuity, and may surface surviving siblings who provide collateral evidence.

**Step 8 — pli_014 · 1900 Census · Ancestry** *(fallback for Step 7)*
Separate Ancestry transcription (NARA T623) as fallback if FamilySearch 1900 is negative.

---

### Fallback map

| Primary | Fallback |
|---------|----------|
| pli_007 (1870 FS — Patrick) | pli_008 (1870 Ancestry — Patrick) |
| pli_010 (1880 FS — Patrick) | pli_011 (1880 Ancestry — Patrick) |
| pli_013 (1900 FS — Patrick) | pli_014 (1900 Ancestry — Patrick) |
| pli_009, pli_012 | *(standalone — no fallback needed)* |

---

### Scope
**8 searches total** — 3 census years × 2 FamilySearch searches each (subject + candidate father), with Ancestry fallbacks on the three Patrick-targeted searches.

---

**Ready to start?** The natural first move is executing **pli_007** (1870 FamilySearch, Patrick Flynn). Say "run the next search" and the search-records skill will take it from there.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: All factual claims are supported. The plan correctly identifies pl_002 as completed, properly infers add-new mode, accurately sequences 8 items across three census years with correct jurisdictions (Schuylkill County, PA). Rationales are grounded in actual historical facts about when relationship columns appeared in censuses (1880 onward) and appropriately reference the input state. No fabricated material or unsupported assertions.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill fully addressed the user's request: created a comprehensive plan for post-1860 federal censuses (1870, 1880, 1900) to track Patrick as an adult and look for Thomas in the same household. All three requested census years are covered with appropriate record type selection (census), fallback chains are explicit, sequencing logic is documented, and ID predictions are provided. No silent omissions of items the input state required.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: The MCP tool call to mcp__genealogy__research_append was syntactically and semantically correct. The nested ops array structure properly appended one plan (pl_003) with correct question_id (q_001) and eight plan items (pli_007–pli_014) with sequential ordering, appropriate record_type values (all 'census'), correct jurisdiction strings, valid date_range values (1870, 1880, 1900), repository values matching FamilySearch/Ancestry expectations, and proper fallback_for linkages. The tool returned success with no fixture_not_found errors.

dimensions.name: Record type selection
dimensions.rationale: Every plan item targets 'census' as the record type, which is the appropriate choice for tracking Patrick as an adult post-1860 and locating Thomas in the same household. Rationales explain the specific information need each census year provides: 1870 establishes adult presence; 1880 is highlighted as the first enumeration with explicit relationship columns (directly advancing toward 'Proved' status); 1900 confirms continued household structure. Record type selection is justified and consistent with the research question's requirements.

dimensions.name: Sequencing logic

---

## Research Plan — pl_002
**Question q_001:** Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, PA, d. 1908)?

**Starting-point note:** The 1850 census places Patrick (age 5) in Thomas Flynn's Schuylkill County household. This is *indirect* evidence of parentage — household position with a shared surname, not a stated relationship. The plan treats Thomas as a *candidate* father and the unknown wife as a *candidate* mother; neither is assumed proven. The plan is designed to confirm, identify, or refute both.

---

### Step 1 — Patrick's 1908 Death Certificate `pli_004`
**Repository:** FamilySearch · *Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906-1968* (coll. 2255317, 4.2M records, fully indexed)
**Target:** 1908, Schuylkill County

Pennsylvania's civil death registration began in 1906 — Patrick died right in the opening years of the system. Death certificates from this era typically name both parents. If the informant had personal knowledge, this is **direct evidence of parentage** and could resolve the question in a single record. Free and fully indexed: search here first.

---

### Step 2 — 1880 Census, Thomas Flynn Household `pli_005`
**Repository:** FamilySearch · US Federal Census 1880 (fully indexed, free)
**Target:** 1880, Schuylkill County

The 1880 census was the first to explicitly record each person's *relationship to the head*. Searching Thomas Flynn (~age 62 in 1880) will reveal: his wife's given name (identifying the mother), any children still at home labeled "son" or "daughter," and whether Patrick (~35) remains in the household. Even if Patrick has his own household by 1880, Thomas's entry identifies the mother and confirms parentage through siblings — stronger indirect evidence than the 1850 census where relationships are inferred.

---

### Step 3 — 1860 Census `pli_006`
**Repository:** FamilySearch · US Federal Census 1860 (fully indexed, free)
**Target:** 1860, Schuylkill County

Patrick would be ~15 in 1860 — almost certainly still in Thomas's household. A second co-enumeration of Patrick and Thomas 10 years on strengthens the cumulative indirect evidence. The 1860 household also shows the wife's name and other children's ages, adding to the picture of the mother's identity.

---

### Step 4 — 1870 Census `pli_007`
**Repository:** FamilySearch · US Federal Census 1870 (fully indexed, free)
**Target:** 1870, Schuylkill County

Patrick would be ~25 in 1870 — possibly still with Thomas (~52) or heading his own household. Searching both Thomas's and Patrick's 1870 entries adds a third census cross-section: another view of the mother's name in Thomas's household, and Patrick's independent biographical baseline for cross-referencing later records.

---

### Step 5 — Thomas Flynn Probate, Indexed `pli_008`
**Repository:** FamilySearch · *Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683-1994* (coll. 1417683, 893K records, indexed)
**Target:** 1851–1900, Schuylkill County

Thomas Flynn (born ~1818) would have died between 1851 and ~1900. A will explicitly naming Patrick as a son is **direct evidence of parentage**. An intestate administration listing heirs is strong indirect evidence. Probate also names the widow, which identifies the mother if she survived Thomas. Search the indexed collection before browsing the unindexed volume.

---

### Step 6 — Thomas Flynn Probate, Browse-Only `pli_009` *(fallback for Step 5)*
**Repository:** FamilySearch · Schuylkill County Probate browse volume (image group 007936749, 1851–1930, 412 images, **0% indexed**)

If the probate index returns nothing for Thomas Flynn, manually browse this Schuylkill-specific volume — it holds records the index missed. This is the safety net before concluding Thomas left no estate record.

---

### Step 7 — Thomas and Wife's Marriage Record `pli_010`
**Repository:** FamilySearch · *Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950* (coll. 1571957) + *Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900* (coll. 1502909)
**Target:** ca. 1835–1848, Pennsylvania

This is a **required dedicated item** for any parentage plan: search for the candidate parents' marriage to *each other*. Thomas (born ~1818) and his wife would have married before Patrick's birth (~1845). If they married in Pennsylvania after immigrating, a record may survive. The marriage supplies the **mother's maiden name** — omitted from every census record — and its date relative to Patrick's birth independently confirms that Thomas was in a position to be Patrick's father.

---

### Step 8 — Church Records `pli_011`
**Repository:** FamilySearch · *Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708-1985* (coll. 1401638, indexed) + Schuylkill browse volume (image group 007936751, 1842–1895, 187 images, **0% indexed**, includes German-language entries)
**Target:** 1842–1895, Schuylkill County

Pennsylvania had no civil vital registration before 1906 — church registers are the primary substitute. The Flynn surname and Irish origin strongly suggest a **Catholic** family. These collections may contain: baptism records for Patrick's younger siblings born in Pennsylvania (which would name Thomas and his wife as parents), a marriage record for Thomas and his wife if they wed locally, and burial entries. The Diocese of Scranton (est. 1868) covers Schuylkill County; its physical archives hold additional sacramental registers not yet fully digitized.

---

### Step 9 — Patrick's Own Marriage Record `pli_012`
**Repository:** FamilySearch · *Pennsylvania, County Marriages, 1885–1950* (coll. 1921317) + Schuylkill browse-only marriage volume (image group 007936753, 1870–1910, 94 images, **0% indexed**)
**Target:** ca. 1865–1900, Schuylkill County

Patrick's own marriage application or license — likely filed ca. 1865–1885 — may name his parents. Late-19th-century Pennsylvania marriage documents increasingly recorded both parties' parents' names. A license naming Thomas Flynn as Patrick's father, or giving the mother's maiden name, would be **direct evidence of parentage from the subject himself**.

---

### Fallback relationships
- Step 6 (probate browse) backs up Step 5 (probate index) — search 6 only if Step 5 is negative.

### Scope
9 searches, all FamilySearch (free). Execution order prioritizes free indexed sources likely to yield the most direct evidence, with browse-only volumes reserved for when indexes come up empty.

---

**Ready to begin?** I can start executing immediately with Step 1 — Patrick's 1908 death certificate on FamilySearch. That single record has the highest probability of resolving the parentage question outright. Would you like me to search it now?

items.rationale: Patrick Flynn died in 1908 in Schuylkill County — squarely within Pennsylvania's civil death registration window (began 1906). The FamilySearch 'Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906-1968' (coll. 2255317, 4.2M records, fully indexed) typically names the decedent's parents, making it potential direct evidence of parentage if the informant had personal knowledge. This is the highest-probability single record available, is free, and should be searched first.

The 1880 census was the first to record each person's explicit relationship to the household head. Searching for Thomas Flynn (born ~1818) in the 1880 Schuylkill County census will reveal: (1) a wife's given name, identifying the mother; (2) remaining children with 'son'/'daughter' relationship labels that corroborate Thomas as Patrick's father; and (3) whether Patrick (~35) is still enumerated in the household. Even if Patrick has his own 1880 household, Thomas's entry would identify the mother and potentially confirm parentage through siblings. This is stronger indirect evidence than the 1850 census because relationships are stated rather than inferred from household position.

In 1860 Patrick would be approximately 15 — almost certainly still in the Thomas Flynn household. A second co-enumeration of Patrick and Thomas strengthens the indirect evidence of parentage; the 1860 household composition (wife's name, other children's ages) may also identify the mother and siblings. Although the 1860 census lacks a relationship column, continued co-residence with consistent ages is meaningful cumulative evidence. FamilySearch has this collection fully indexed and free.

By 1870 Patrick would be ~25 — possibly heading his own household or still with Thomas (~52). Searching Thomas's 1870 household adds another view of a wife (the likely mother) and remaining children. Searching for Patrick's own 1870 entry establishes his biographical position at that point and confirms continued Pennsylvania residence, enabling cross-referencing with later records.

Thomas Flynn (born ~1818) would have died sometime between 1851 and ~1900. The FamilySearch 'Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683-1994' (coll. 1417683, 893K records, indexed) covers Schuylkill County. A will explicitly naming Patrick as a son is direct evidence of parentage; an intestate administration listing heirs is indirect but compelling. Probate also reveals the mother's name if Thomas predeceased her and she is named as widow. Search the indexed collection before the unindexed browse volume (item 6).

The Schuylkill County probate browse-only volume (image group 007936749, 1851-1930, 412 images, 0% indexed) holds records not captured in the FamilySearch probate index. If item 5 returns no result for Thomas Flynn, manually browse this volume to check for entries the index missed. This is the fallback source for Thomas's estate records.

A dedicated search for the candidate parents' marriage to each other is required for a parentage question. Thomas Flynn (born ~1818) and his wife would have married before Patrick's birth (~1845), most likely 1835-1848. If they married in Pennsylvania after immigrating from Ireland, the record may appear in 'Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677-1950' (coll. 1571957) or 'Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700-1900' (coll. 1502909). The marriage record directly supplies the mother's maiden name — information omitted from all census records — and its date relative to Patrick's birth provides independent confirmation that Thomas was in a position to be Patrick's father.

Pennsylvania civil registration did not begin until 1906, making church registers the primary pre-1906 vital record substitute. The Flynn surname and Irish origin strongly suggest a Catholic family. The FamilySearch indexed collection 'Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708-1985' (coll. 1401638) and the Schuylkill-specific browse-only volume (image group 007936751, 1842-1895, 187 images, 0% indexed, includes German-language entries) may contain: baptism records for Patrick's younger siblings born in Pennsylvania (naming Thomas and his wife as parents), a marriage record for Thomas and his wife if they married locally, and confirmation or burial records. The Diocese of Scranton (est. 1868) covers Schuylkill County and its archives hold sacramental registers not yet fully digitized.

Patrick Flynn's own marriage record — likely ca. 1865-1885 given his ~1845 birth — may name his parents. Pennsylvania marriage applications and licenses from the late 19th century increasingly recorded both parties' parents' names. The FamilySearch 'Pennsylvania, County Marriages, 1885-1950' (coll. 1921317) and the Schuylkill browse-only marriage volume (image group 007936753, 1870-1910, 94 images, 0% indexed) are the relevant repositories. A marriage application naming Thomas Flynn as Patrick's father, or giving the mother's maiden name, would be direct evidence of parentage from the subject himself.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: All outputs are factually accurate and well-supported. The plan correctly identifies Patrick Flynn's parentage question, cites evidence from the locality survey (Pennsylvania civil registration 1906, church records, Diocese of Scranton 1868, 1880 census relationship column, probate collections 1683-1994), and proposes 9 plan items with justified record types. Jurisdictions are correct (Schuylkill County formed 1811+), dates align with available records, and all rationales cite specific FamilySearch collections by ID and record counts from the survey responses.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed every requirement: performed a full locality survey (place_search, collections_search, wiki pages, external_links, volume_search), produced a complete 9-item plan with specific sequences, repositories, date ranges, and detailed rationales, created the plan in research.json with correct schema, and delivered the plan to the user. No silent omissions—all required components are present.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All 6 MCP tool calls passed correct arguments. place_search received 'Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania' (matches ~Schuylkill); collections_search received Pennsylvania statewide with 1840-1910 date range; wiki_place_page queries were correctly parameterized for 'Pennsylvania' and 'research_tips'/'online_records' sections; external_links_search and volume_search both received Schuylkill with appropriate date ranges; research_append correctly batched 9 plan item insertions with planId 'pl_002'. No fixture_not_found errors; all calls matched successfully.

dimensions.name: Record type selection
dimensions.rationale: Every plan item targets appropriate record types tied to the parentage question. Vital records (death certificate, marriage records) seek direct evidence; census items (1880 with relationship column, 1860, 1870, 1850 baseline) establish co-residence and family structure; probate seeks testamentary naming or heir lists; church records substitute for pre-1906 civil registration. Each rationale explicitly states what specific information the record type provides (e.g., '1880 census was first to record relationship to head,' 'death certificates typically name parents,' 'probate reveals widow = mother'). No generic rationales; all are tailored to parentage evidence.

dimensions.name: Sequencing logic
dimensions.rationale: Plan items are logically ordered: items 1-4 prioritize free, indexed sources (death certificate, census records 1880/1860/1870) likely to yield direct or strong indirect evidence; item 5 targets indexed probate (free); item 6 explicitly backs up item 5 with fallback_for reference to pli_008 (browse-only volume only if index fails); items 7-9 pursue specialized records (marriage, church, Patrick's own marriage). Free sources precede unindexed; highest-probability single record (1908 death) is first. Fallback chain is explicit and defensible.

dimensions.name: Jurisdiction accuracy
dimensions.rationale: All jurisdictions are historically accurate for the 1840-1910 period. Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania is correctly identified as formed 1811+ (confirmed by survey response dateRange '+1811/'). Plan items specify Schuylkill County for local records (censuses, probate, church) and Pennsylvania-statewide for collections available across the state (marriages 1835-1848, death certificates 1906+). Church records note Diocese of Scranton (est. 1868, confirmed in wiki response) covers Schuylkill post-1868. No anachronistic jurisdictions or boundary errors.

dimensions.name: Plan mode and lifecycle
dimensions.rationale: This is the first plan for q_001 (per test context: 'NO plan yet'). The skill correctly invoked 'add-new' mode: no prior active plan exists for this question, so no supersede or review was needed. The plan was created fresh with status='active', proper ID sequencing (pl_002 following pl_001 from q_002), and correct created timestamp. Only one active plan exists for q_001 after execution. The audit trail is preserved: pl_001 (for q_002) remains untouched. Lifecycle is clean and correct.

dimensions.name: Locality survey integration
dimensions.rationale: Plan explicitly cites at least three pieces of evidence from the locality survey: (1) Pennsylvania civil registration began 1906 (from wiki and collections search), (2) Diocese of Scranton established 1868 (from wiki online_records section), (3) 1880 census introduced relationship-to-head column (from wiki research_tips), (4) browse-only volumes with specific image groups and coverage dates (from volume_search: probate 007936749 1851-1930, church 007936751 1842-1895), (5) probate records 1683-1994 indexed (from collections_search). Each plan item references survey discoveries; the plan is grounded in locality evidence, not generic assumptions.

Here is **Plan pl_003** for q_001 — *"Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, PA, d. 1908)?"*

---

## Research Plan pl_003 — Parentage of Patrick Flynn

**Why a new plan?** pl_002 is completed. The proof summary stands at *probable*, not *proved*: both census records provide only indirect parentage evidence (1850/1860 censuses do not state relationships), the death certificate informant is secondary, and several high-value record types were never searched.

**Key planning observations:**
- **Patrick's mother is entirely unidentified.** No record found so far names her. Her maiden name must be the plan's primary secondary objective.
- The prior probate search (pli_006) hit only the *indexed* FamilySearch collection and returned negative — a browse-only film covering the same county was never touched.
- Schuylkill County's Irish Catholic coal-mining community has a rich set of unindexed browse-only volumes on FamilySearch that pl_002 did not reach.

---

### Execution order

| # | pli | Record type | What to look for | Why now |
|---|-----|-------------|-----------------|---------|
| 1 | pli_007 | **1870 Census** (FamilySearch, indexed) | Thomas Flynn household; *if Patrick's mother is still living, her name appears here* | Free, indexed, high probability; narrows Thomas's death window |
| 2 | pli_008 | **1880 Census** (FamilySearch, indexed) | Relationship-to-head column; parents' birthplaces column | **First census with direct parent–child evidence**; parents' birthplace field may reveal Irish county |
| 3 | pli_009 | **1900 Census** (FamilySearch, indexed) | Patrick in own household; father's/mother's birthplace columns | May report Irish county of origin even if Thomas is deceased |
| 4 | pli_010 | **Parents' marriage — church records, indexed** (PA Church & Town Records 1708-1985, collection 1401638) | Thomas Flynn + wife's marriage; mother's maiden name | Dedicated parents'-marriage item; supplies maiden name absent from every record so far |
| 5 | pli_011 | **Parents' marriage — church records, browse-only** (imageGroup 007936751, 1842-1895) | Flynn baptisms, marriages, burials in unindexed Catholic parish films | *Fallback for pli_010*; 0% indexed, contains records the indexed collection misses |
| 6 | pli_012 | **Naturalization records, browse-only** (imageGroup 007936750, 1849-1915) | Thomas or Patrick Flynn's declaration of intent; Irish county of origin | Pre-1906 declarations often name the specific Irish county — the key to Irish records |
| 7 | pli_013 | **Probate, browse-only volume** (imageGroup 007936749, 1851-1930) | Thomas Flynn's will or letters of administration, naming children | Extends the prior negative indexed search (pli_006) to unindexed film |
| 8 | pli_014 | **Deed records** (imageGroup 007936752, 1815-1900, 22% indexed) | Thomas Flynn as grantor — wife's dower-rights signature names her | Under PA law the wife must sign; deed may name Patrick's mother even if she pre-deceased the 1870 census |
| 9 | pli_015 | **FAN cluster — neighboring households** (1850 & 1860 census, FamilySearch) | Other Flynn families in dwellings near 84 (1850) and 112 (1860) | BCG Std. 14; Irish neighbors from same townland → their records may name Thomas's wife or Irish origin |

**Fallback relationship:** If pli_010 (indexed church records) yields nothing → execute pli_011 (browse-only church film).

**Total searches:** 9 items across 4 record types (census, church/vital, court, land/probate).

---

### What a successful plan would achieve

- **1870 or 1880 census positive** → mother's given name identified; 1880 relationship field → direct parentage evidence → conclusion advances to *proved*
- **Parents' marriage record found** → mother's full maiden name; marriage date confirms paternity
- **Naturalization declaration found** → Irish county of origin → enables a future Irish-records plan for baptism and marriage
- **Browse-only probate found** → will naming Patrick as son → direct parentage evidence independent of secondary-informant death certificate

---

Ready to start executing? Say **"run the next search"** and I'll begin pli_007 (1870 census on FamilySearch).

args.query: Pennsylvania Catholic church records Irish immigrants Schuylkill County genealogy

entry.rationale: The 1870 census would locate Thomas Flynn (born ~1818, age ~52) if still living, and may show Patrick (~age 25) still in or near the household. Critically, if Patrick's mother survived to 1870, she would appear here by name — every record found so far names only Thomas as father, and the mother remains entirely unidentified. Finding the 1870 household either supplies her given name directly or narrows Thomas's death window for the browse-only probate search (pli_013). Indexed on FamilySearch; free access.

The 1880 census is the first to record the relationship to the head of household in a dedicated column, making it the earliest census capable of providing direct evidence of a parent-child relationship. If Patrick (age ~35) appears in Thomas Flynn's 1880 household, the relationship field would constitute direct evidence of parentage from an original source — upgrading the conclusion from 'probable' toward 'proved.' Additionally, the 1880 census records each person's father's birthplace and mother's birthplace, which could identify the Irish county of origin for Thomas and his wife — essential for locating the parents' marriage record and Patrick's baptism in Ireland. Note: the 1890 census is largely destroyed and is not planned.

Patrick Flynn (age ~55) would appear in his own 1900 household, eight years before his death. The 1900 census asks for each person's birthplace and their father's and mother's birthplaces; even if Thomas Flynn is deceased by 1900, Patrick may report his parents' Irish county of origin. This column is critical because no record found so far identifies which county in Ireland the Flynns came from, and that information is required to access Irish baptism and marriage registers. The 1900 census also records how many years a wife has been married, enabling cross-checks on the family timeline. Indexed on FamilySearch; free access.

Dedicated parents'-marriage search required for every parentage plan: Thomas Flynn and his wife (Patrick's unknown mother) were Irish Catholic immigrants settled in Schuylkill County by 1850. As Catholics in a coal-mining community they would have registered sacraments at a local parish such as St. Patrick's, Pottsville. The FamilySearch indexed collection 'Pennsylvania Church and Town Records, 1708-1985' (collection 1401638) is the first access point. A church marriage record for Thomas Flynn would: (a) confirm the couple as a married unit; (b) supply the mother's maiden name, absent from every record found so far; and (c) by its date relative to Patrick's birth ca. 1845, provide direct evidence of paternity, able to confirm or refute Thomas as father even where the death certificate informant is a secondary source. Even a burial record for the wife in a Schuylkill County Catholic register would supply her surname. Note: if the marriage pre-dated immigration (likely, given Patrick was born in Ireland ca. 1845), Irish records will require a separate plan once the county of origin is identified via the 1880/1900 census or naturalization records.

Fallback for pli_010 if the indexed Pennsylvania Church and Town Records return no Thomas Flynn result. The Schuylkill County Church Records browse-only volume (imageGroup 007936751, 1842-1895, FamilySearch) is 0% indexed and covers the critical immigration-and-settlement period; it contains records the indexed collection does not capture. Browsing this film for Irish Catholic parishes in the county (St. Patrick's Pottsville, Assumption of BVM Pottsville, St. Joseph's Minersville, and others) may yield a Flynn family marriage, baptism, or burial record naming Patrick's mother by her maiden name.

Pre-1906 naturalizations in Pennsylvania occurred in any court of record, most commonly the county Quarter Sessions Court. Thomas Flynn and/or Patrick Flynn would have naturalized in Schuylkill County. Naturalization declarations of intent (first papers) sometimes name the declarant's specific town and county of origin in Ireland, the ship and port of arrival, and arrival date. This Irish-origin detail is the key to locating the parents' marriage and Patrick's baptism in Irish parish registers — without the county, those records are unsearchable by name. The Schuylkill County Naturalization Records browse-only volume (imageGroup 007936750, 1849-1915, FamilySearch) is 0% indexed and must be browsed. Post-1906 naturalizations (if applicable) are held by NARA Philadelphia.

The prior probate search (pli_006, log_006) searched FamilySearch's indexed Pennsylvania Probate Records collection (collection 1417683) and returned negative. However, the Schuylkill County Probate Records browse-only volume (imageGroup 007936749, 1851-1930, FamilySearch) is 0% indexed — Thomas Flynn's will or letters of administration may exist only in these unindexed images, unreachable by the prior keyword search. Browsing the Register of Wills index pages for surnames in the F- range would either locate Thomas Flynn's estate record (a will naming his children as legatees would be direct parentage evidence) or confirm he died intestate or outside the county.

The Schuylkill County Deed Records volume (imageGroup 007936752, 1815-1900, FamilySearch, 22% indexed) may show Thomas Flynn as grantor or grantee. Under Pennsylvania law, a married grantor's wife was required to sign the deed and relinquish her dower rights — her given name (and sometimes her maiden name) appears directly in the deed text, making deed records a primary source for identifying a wife who does not appear in surviving census or church records. This is one of the few record types that could name Patrick's mother even if she died before the 1870 census. Deed witnesses also typically came from the same community and may be Flynn relatives. A partial index exists (22%); browsing the F- sections of the grantee/grantor indexes is required for full coverage.

FAN-cluster search (BCG Standard 14): the 1850 census places Thomas Flynn at dwelling 84 and the 1860 census at dwelling 112. Neighboring households in both enumerations should be examined for other Flynn families — brothers, cousins, or in-laws of Thomas — who may share an Irish origin and appear in records naming their common parentage or home county. Irish immigrants from the same townland routinely settled in the same coal-mining community; other Flynns who arrived from the same Irish county would be the most likely neighbors. Their naturalization declarations, church records, or probate records may name Thomas Flynn's wife, parents, or Irish county of origin, providing parentage evidence through collateral relatives when direct-line records are silent.

entry.rationale: The 1870 census would locate Thomas Flynn (born ~1818, age ~52) if still living, and may show Patrick (~age 25) still in or near the household. Critically, if Patrick's mother survived to 1870, she would appear here by name — every record found so far names only Thomas as father, and the mother remains entirely unidentified. Finding the 1870 household either supplies her given name directly or narrows Thomas's death window for the browse-only probate search (pli_013). Indexed on FamilySearch; free access.

The 1880 census is the first to record the relationship to the head of household in a dedicated column, making it the earliest census capable of providing direct evidence of a parent-child relationship. If Patrick (age ~35) appears in Thomas Flynn's 1880 household, the relationship field would constitute direct evidence of parentage from an original source — upgrading the conclusion from 'probable' toward 'proved.' Additionally, the 1880 census records each person's father's birthplace and mother's birthplace, which could identify the Irish county of origin for Thomas and his wife — essential for locating the parents' marriage record and Patrick's baptism in Ireland. Note: the 1890 census is largely destroyed and is not planned.

Patrick Flynn (age ~55) would appear in his own 1900 household, eight years before his death. The 1900 census asks for each person's birthplace and their father's and mother's birthplaces; even if Thomas Flynn is deceased by 1900, Patrick may report his parents' Irish county of origin. This column is critical because no record found so far identifies which county in Ireland the Flynns came from, and that information is required to access Irish baptism and marriage registers. The 1900 census also records how many years a wife has been married, enabling cross-checks on the family timeline. Indexed on FamilySearch; free access.

Dedicated parents'-marriage search required for every parentage plan: Thomas Flynn and his wife (Patrick's unknown mother) were Irish Catholic immigrants settled in Schuylkill County by 1850. As Catholics in a coal-mining community they would have registered sacraments at a local parish such as St. Patrick's, Pottsville. The FamilySearch indexed collection 'Pennsylvania Church and Town Records, 1708-1985' (collection 1401638) is the first access point. A church marriage record for Thomas Flynn would: (a) confirm the couple as a married unit; (b) supply the mother's maiden name, absent from every record found so far; and (c) by its date relative to Patrick's birth ca. 1845, provide direct evidence of paternity, able to confirm or refute Thomas as father even where the death certificate informant is a secondary source. Even a burial record for the wife in a Schuylkill County Catholic register would supply her surname. Note: if the marriage pre-dated immigration (likely, given Patrick was born in Ireland ca. 1845), Irish records will require a separate plan once the county of origin is identified via the 1880/1900 census or naturalization records.

Fallback for pli_010 if the indexed Pennsylvania Church and Town Records return no Thomas Flynn result. The Schuylkill County Church Records browse-only volume (imageGroup 007936751, 1842-1895, FamilySearch) is 0% indexed and covers the critical immigration-and-settlement period; it contains records the indexed collection does not capture. Browsing this film for Irish Catholic parishes in the county (St. Patrick's Pottsville, Assumption of BVM Pottsville, St. Joseph's Minersville, and others) may yield a Flynn family marriage, baptism, or burial record naming Patrick's mother by her maiden name.

Pre-1906 naturalizations in Pennsylvania occurred in any court of record, most commonly the county Quarter Sessions Court. Thomas Flynn and/or Patrick Flynn would have naturalized in Schuylkill County. Naturalization declarations of intent (first papers) sometimes name the declarant's specific town and county of origin in Ireland, the ship and port of arrival, and arrival date. This Irish-origin detail is the key to locating the parents' marriage and Patrick's baptism in Irish parish registers — without the county, those records are unsearchable by name. The Schuylkill County Naturalization Records browse-only volume (imageGroup 007936750, 1849-1915, FamilySearch) is 0% indexed and must be browsed. Post-1906 naturalizations (if applicable) are held by NARA Philadelphia.

The prior probate search (pli_006, log_006) searched FamilySearch's indexed Pennsylvania Probate Records collection (collection 1417683) and returned negative. However, the Schuylkill County Probate Records browse-only volume (imageGroup 007936749, 1851-1930, FamilySearch) is 0% indexed — Thomas Flynn's will or letters of administration may exist only in these unindexed images, unreachable by the prior keyword search. Browsing the Register of Wills index pages for surnames in the F- range would either locate Thomas Flynn's estate record (a will naming his children as legatees would be direct parentage evidence) or confirm he died intestate or outside the county.

The Schuylkill County Deed Records volume (imageGroup 007936752, 1815-1900, FamilySearch, 22% indexed) may show Thomas Flynn as grantor or grantee. Under Pennsylvania law, a married grantor's wife was required to sign the deed and relinquish her dower rights — her given name (and sometimes her maiden name) appears directly in the deed text, making deed records a primary source for identifying a wife who does not appear in surviving census or church records. This is one of the few record types that could name Patrick's mother even if she died before the 1870 census. Deed witnesses also typically came from the same community and may be Flynn relatives. A partial index exists (22%); browsing the F- sections of the grantee/grantor indexes is required for full coverage.

FAN-cluster search (BCG Standard 14): the 1850 census places Thomas Flynn at dwelling 84 and the 1860 census at dwelling 112. Neighboring households in both enumerations should be examined for other Flynn families — brothers, cousins, or in-laws of Thomas — who may share an Irish origin and appear in records naming their common parentage or home county. Irish immigrants from the same townland routinely settled in the same coal-mining community; other Flynns who arrived from the same Irish county would be the most likely neighbors. Their naturalization declarations, church records, or probate records may name Thomas Flynn's wife, parents, or Irish county of origin, providing parentage evidence through collateral relatives when direct-line records are silent.

entry.rationale: The baptism register entry for Mikkel's birth (20 Aug 1779) should name the father directly. Search the indexed portion of 'Denmark, Church Books, 1484–1941' (collection 2078555) for a baptism in Stagstrup around August 1779. A baptism record is direct evidence of parentage and is the highest-probability first source.

The Stagstrup kirkebog image group (004130616, 1763–1814) is only 3% indexed, so an indexed search will likely miss the entry. Browse the register images directly for the August 1779 baptism section. Rigsarkivet arkivalieronline also provides free access to the same images. Fallback for item 1.

The 1787 Danish national census is fully searchable on FamilySearch (collection 1401625) and via Dansk Demografisk Database. Mikkel would be approximately 8 years old and almost certainly still in his father's household. The census records all household members with ages and relationships, providing indirect evidence of the father's name, age, and occupation — and independently corroborating the baptism.

The military levying rolls (lægdsruller) for Thisted Amt (image group 004748896) enroll Mikkel from approximately 1789 (age ~10). By convention the father's name is written directly above the son's entry — making this direct parentage evidence. Notations such as 'gl. M. S.' (old man's son) or 'GBES' (widow's son) can bracket the father's death between sessions. The volume is 0% indexed; browse-only on FamilySearch and free via Rigsarkivet arkivalieronline. Per Danish research guidance, lægdsruller rank alongside the baptism register as primary parentage evidence for male subjects.

Search the Stagstrup kirkebog (collection 2078555 and image group 004130616) for the marriage of Mikkel's parents — a 'Niels [surname]' marrying Mikkel's mother, likely between c.1760 and 1779. The marriage register supplies the mother's maiden name (absent from census and death records) and, by its date relative to Mikkel's birth, provides corroborating direct evidence confirming this Niels as the father. A dedicated parentage plan requires the parents' own marriage record per GPS standards.

If Mikkel's father Niels died leaving an estate, the skifteprotokol (probate record) for Hassing Herred would name the widow and all heirs — including Mikkel — with their residences, providing strong kinship evidence of parentage. Search the indexed 'Denmark, Probate Indexes, 1674–1851' (collection 2015318) first; then browse image group 004392710 (Hassing Herred, 1690–1850) if no indexed entry is found.

entry.rationale: The baptism register entry for Mikkel's birth (20 Aug 1779) should name the father directly. Search the indexed portion of 'Denmark, Church Books, 1484–1941' (collection 2078555) for a baptism in Stagstrup around August 1779. A baptism record is direct evidence of parentage and is the highest-probability first source.

The Stagstrup kirkebog image group (004130616, 1763–1814) is only 3% indexed, so an indexed search will likely miss the entry. Browse the register images directly for the August 1779 baptism section. Rigsarkivet arkivalieronline also provides free access to the same images. Fallback for item 1.

The 1787 Danish national census is fully searchable on FamilySearch (collection 1401625) and via Dansk Demografisk Database. Mikkel would be approximately 8 years old and almost certainly still in his father's household. The census records all household members with ages and relationships, providing indirect evidence of the father's name, age, and occupation — and independently corroborating the baptism.

The military levying rolls (lægdsruller) for Thisted Amt (image group 004748896) enroll Mikkel from approximately 1789 (age ~10). By convention the father's name is written directly above the son's entry — making this direct parentage evidence. Notations such as 'gl. M. S.' (old man's son) or 'GBES' (widow's son) can bracket the father's death between sessions. The volume is 0% indexed; browse-only on FamilySearch and free via Rigsarkivet arkivalieronline. Per Danish research guidance, lægdsruller rank alongside the baptism register as primary parentage evidence for male subjects.

Search the Stagstrup kirkebog (collection 2078555 and image group 004130616) for the marriage of Mikkel's parents — a 'Niels [surname]' marrying Mikkel's mother, likely between c.1760 and 1779. The marriage register supplies the mother's maiden name (absent from census and death records) and, by its date relative to Mikkel's birth, provides corroborating direct evidence confirming this Niels as the father. A dedicated parentage plan requires the parents' own marriage record per GPS standards.

If Mikkel's father Niels died leaving an estate, the skifteprotokol (probate record) for Hassing Herred would name the widow and all heirs — including Mikkel — with their residences, providing strong kinship evidence of parentage. Search the indexed 'Denmark, Probate Indexes, 1674–1851' (collection 2015318) first; then browse image group 004392710 (Hassing Herred, 1690–1850) if no indexed entry is found.

## Research Plan pl_002 · q_001 — Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn?

**Starting-point note:** The sole current evidence that Thomas Flynn (I2) is Patrick's father is indirect — household co-enumeration in the 1850 census. Patrick's mother has no name anywhere in the research. The three records referenced in the tree (1860 census S2, death certificate S3, probate S4) were never formally searched or extracted through the research workflow; this plan formalises that work and extends the search topically.

---

### Execution order

| # | ID | Record type | Jurisdiction | Period | Repository |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | pli_004 | **Vital record — death certificate** | Schuylkill Co., PA | 1908 | FamilySearch |
| 2 | pli_005 | **Census — 1860** | Schuylkill Co., PA | 1860 | FamilySearch |
| 3 | pli_006 | **Probate** | Schuylkill Co., PA | 1860–1910 | FamilySearch |
| 4 | pli_007 | **Church — Thomas & wife's marriage** | Schuylkill Co., PA | 1840–1860 | FamilySearch |
| 5 | pli_008 | **Census — 1870** | Schuylkill Co., PA | 1870 | FamilySearch |
| 6 | pli_009 | **Census — 1880** | Schuylkill Co., PA | 1880 | FamilySearch |
| 7 | pli_010 | **Court — naturalization** | Schuylkill Co., PA | 1845–1880 | FamilySearch |
| 8 | pli_011 | **Immigration — passenger records** | Atlantic ports | 1840–1850 | FamilySearch |
| 9 | pli_012 | Vital record — death certificate *(fallback for 1)* | Pennsylvania | 1908 | Ancestry |
| 10 | pli_013 | Church *(fallback for 4)* | Schuylkill Co., PA | 1840–1870 | other (Diocese of Scranton) |

---

### Rationales in execution order

**1 · pli_004 — Patrick's 1908 death certificate (FamilySearch)**
Patrick died 12 March 1908; Pennsylvania required death registration from 1906, so a certificate exists. FamilySearch's *Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906–1968* (collection 2255317) is indexed. A 1908 certificate typically carries a "parents of decedent" section — **the single highest-value item** for directly answering the question. The certificate appears as S3 in the tree supporting relationship R1 but has never been searched or extracted through the research workflow.

**2 · pli_005 — 1860 federal census (FamilySearch)**
Patrick would be ~15 in 1860 and very likely still in his parents' household. Finding him co-enumerated with Thomas Flynn **and a wife** would name Patrick's mother for the first time — the most critical gap in current knowledge. Referenced as S2 in the tree for R1 but never formally logged or extracted.

**3 · pli_006 — Schuylkill County probate / Thomas Flynn's will (FamilySearch)**
A will naming Patrick as a son is **direct** parentage evidence. Thomas (born ~1818) could have died any time between ~1860 and 1910. Two FamilySearch access paths exist: indexed collection 1417683 and browse-only image volume 007936749 (Schuylkill Co. probate 1851–1930, 412 images, 0% indexed). Probate Records appear as S4 in the tree but were never formally extracted.

**4 · pli_007 — Marriage of Thomas Flynn & wife, Catholic church records (FamilySearch)**
*(Dedicated parentage-plan item required by BCG planning standards.)* The couple's marriage record supplies the mother's maiden name — which census and death records usually omit — and proves the couple was married before Patrick's ~1845 birth. The Irish Catholic community in Schuylkill County's coal region makes Catholic registers the most likely source. FamilySearch has collection 1401638 (indexed) plus browse-only volume 007936751 (Schuylkill Co. church records 1842–1895, 187 images, English/German, 0% indexed).

**5 · pli_008 — 1870 federal census (FamilySearch)**
Tracks Thomas Flynn at ~52 and Patrick at ~25. If Thomas is still head of household, his wife's name appears again. If Thomas has died, the absence informs the probate search. Bridges the census sequence toward 1880.

**6 · pli_009 — 1880 federal census (FamilySearch)**
First census with an explicit relationship-to-head column. Patrick (~35) in his own household reports his parents' birthplaces. Thomas (~62) may still be alive. Siblings still at home corroborate the parentage hypothesis. The 1890 census is largely destroyed, making 1880 the last pre-gap enumeration.

**7 · pli_010 — Schuylkill County naturalization records, Thomas Flynn (FamilySearch)**
Pre-1906 naturalizations were in county courts (prothonotary/Quarter Sessions). Browse-only volume 007936750 covers Schuylkill Co. 1849–1915 (298 images, 0% indexed). Thomas's declaration of intention or petition may name his wife and children, and may state his Irish county of origin — opening a path to Irish parish records if U.S. records prove insufficient.

**8 · pli_011 — Immigration / passenger records, Thomas Flynn family (FamilySearch)**
Thomas emigrated from Ireland before 1850. A manifest may list the family unit — wife by name and children, confirming whether Patrick was born before or after emigration. A nil result resolves the birthplace discrepancy (census says Ireland; tree project notes say Pennsylvania).

**9 · pli_012 — Ancestry: Pennsylvania Death Certificates (fallback for pli_004)**
If FamilySearch's indexed collection fails to surface Patrick's certificate or returns an illegible image, Ancestry's independent index (*Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906–1963*) provides an alternate access path to the same certificate.

**10 · pli_013 — Diocese of Scranton Archives (fallback for pli_007)**
If FamilySearch church records don't include the relevant Schuylkill County Catholic parish, the Diocese of Scranton Archives (est. 1868) holds sacramental registers transferred from the Philadelphia Diocese. These may contain baptismal and marriage registers naming Thomas Flynn's wife.

---

### Fallback relationships
- If **step 1** (FamilySearch death cert) yields nothing → **step 9** (Ancestry death cert)
- If **step 4** (FamilySearch church records) yields no marriage → **step 10** (Diocese of Scranton Archives)

### Scope
**10 searches** across 6 record types (vital, census ×3, probate, church ×2, court, immigration, church/external) and 3 repositories (FamilySearch ×8, Ancestry ×1, other ×1). Steps 1–8 are free on FamilySearch; steps 9–10 are fallbacks at external repositories.

---

**Ready to start?** The logical first step is executing **pli_004** — Patrick's 1908 death certificate on FamilySearch. Shall I run that search now?
