items.rationale: Patrick Flynn died 12 March 1908. Pennsylvania required death registration from 1906, so a certificate must exist. FamilySearch holds 'Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906-1968' (collection 2255317), an indexed collection. A 1908 Pennsylvania death certificate typically carries an informant field and a parents-of-decedent section — this is the single highest-value record for directly answering the parentage question. The certificate is referenced as S3 in the tree for the parent-child relationship with Thomas Flynn (R1), but no log entry or assertion derived from it exists in the research workflow; formal search and extraction are needed.

Patrick would be approximately 15 in 1860 and likely still residing in his parents' household. The 1860 census lists all household members by name; finding Patrick co-enumerated with Thomas Flynn and a wife would name Patrick's mother for the first time and confirm the family structure as indirect evidence of parentage. The 1860 census is referenced as S2 in the tree supporting the parent-child relationship R1, but no log entry or assertion from this record exists in the research workflow. Free on FamilySearch; search before Ancestry.

A will or letters of administration for Thomas Flynn naming Patrick as a son would be direct evidence of parentage. Thomas was born ca. 1818 and plausibly died and left a probate record between 1860 and 1910. FamilySearch has two access paths: the indexed 'Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683-1994' (collection 1417683) and a browse-only image volume for Schuylkill County 1851-1930 (image group 007936749, 412 images, 0% indexed). Schuylkill County Probate Records appear as S4 in the tree, suggesting a relevant document was identified by a prior researcher but not formally extracted through the research workflow.

Dedicated item for the marriage of Thomas Flynn to Patrick's mother, required for any parentage plan. The couple's marriage record would directly supply the mother's maiden name — information no census or death record is likely to give — and establishes the marriage date relative to Patrick's ca. 1845 birth, providing direct evidence of their relationship as a couple before Patrick was born. The Flynn family appears to be Irish Catholic (Irish-born, Schuylkill County coal-mining region). FamilySearch holds 'Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708-1985' (collection 1401638, 1.2M records) and a browse-only volume of Schuylkill County church records 1842-1895 (image group 007936751, 187 images, 0% indexed, English and German). Patrick's mother's maiden name is not documented anywhere in the current research.

Patrick (~25 in 1870) may remain in the Thomas Flynn household or be traceable nearby. Finding Thomas in 1870 establishes whether Patrick's mother was still living, extends the household co-enumeration evidence, and helps narrow the window in which Thomas likely died — informing the probate date-range search. Complements the 1860 search and bridges to 1880. Free on FamilySearch.

The 1880 census introduces a relationship-to-head column, making family reconstruction explicit rather than inferred from position. Patrick (~35) in his own household would still report his parents' birthplaces. Thomas Flynn (~62) may still be alive and enumerable. The 1880 census can also reveal siblings still at home whose existence corroborates the Thomas Flynn parentage hypothesis. Note: the 1890 census is largely destroyed, so 1880 is the last pre-gap enumeration. Free on FamilySearch.

Thomas Flynn (born ca. 1818, Ireland) was in Pennsylvania by 1850. Pre-1906 Pennsylvania naturalizations occurred in county courts (prothonotary or Quarter Sessions). A browse-only volume of Schuylkill County Naturalization Records 1849-1915 exists on FamilySearch (image group 007936750, 298 images, 0% indexed). Thomas's declaration of intention or final papers may name his wife and children — providing another path to the mother's name — and his Irish county of origin, which could enable follow-on research in Irish parish records.

Thomas Flynn (born ca. 1818, Ireland) emigrated before 1850. A passenger manifest may list the family unit — wife by name and any children, including Patrick if born before emigration. FamilySearch has indexed Atlantic-port passenger manifests for this period. Even a nil result is informative: if no Thomas Flynn family is found in this window, Patrick was likely born in Pennsylvania despite the 1850 census reporting Ireland as his birthplace.

Fallback for item 1 (pli_004). Ancestry holds an independent index of 'Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906-1963.' If the FamilySearch search fails to surface Patrick's certificate or yields an illegible image, Ancestry's index provides an alternate access path to the same underlying record.

Fallback for item 4 (pli_007). The Diocese of Scranton Archives (dioceseofscranton.org) holds Catholic sacramental registers for Schuylkill County. Pre-1868 records were under the Philadelphia Diocese; the Scranton Archives has custody of registers transferred at the 1868 split. If the FamilySearch church collection does not include the relevant Schuylkill County Catholic parish, the diocesan archives may hold baptismal registers naming Patrick's parents and marriage registers with the mother's maiden name.

The active plan is **pl_002** for the parentage question (q_001). Two of its three items are done; the next step is in progress on probate records but names no specific FamilySearch collection. Let me confirm what's actually available before presenting.Here is a full review of the active research plan for the parentage question.

---

## Research Plan — pl_002
**Question (q_001):** Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, PA, d. 1908)?

---

### Step 1 — 1860 Census, Schuylkill County, PA · FamilySearch ✅ *Completed*
Patrick Flynn (age 15) found again in Thomas Flynn's household in Schuylkill County, dwelling 112. Confirms he remained in the same household a decade later — strengthening the parent-child inference from 1850.

### Step 2 — Death Certificate (1908), Schuylkill County, PA · FamilySearch ✅ *Completed*
Patrick's death certificate (no. 4521) located. Informant James Brown (son-in-law) named **Thomas Flynn as father** — direct evidence of parentage, though secondary because Brown was not a witness to Patrick's birth.

### Step 3 — Probate Records, Schuylkill County, PA · FamilySearch 🔄 *In Progress — next step*
**Rationale:** A will or administration for Thomas Flynn naming Patrick as a son would add a third, independent line of direct parentage evidence — and from a primary informant (Thomas himself), unlike the death certificate.

**Confirmed collection:** *Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994* (FamilySearch collection 1417683) — 2.3 million images, covers 1870–1890. **Note:** `personCount = 0` indicates this collection is **image-only, not indexed**. The search will require browsing the Schuylkill County probate volumes directly rather than running a name search.

---

### Plan at a Glance

| # | Record Type | Repository | Status |
|---|-------------|-----------|--------|
| pli_004 | 1860 Census | FamilySearch | ✅ Completed |
| pli_005 | Death Certificate (1908) | FamilySearch | ✅ Completed |
| pli_006 | Probate (1870–1890) | FamilySearch | 🔄 In Progress |

**3 items total — 2 completed, 1 in progress.** No fallback items are currently planned for the probate step; if the browse search yields nothing, an Ancestry probate search or a direct county courthouse inquiry would be natural next steps.

---

### Where Things Stand
The hypothesis that Thomas Flynn is Patrick's father is currently rated **Probable** — supported by indirect census co-residence (1850, 1860) plus one direct but secondary-informant source (the death certificate). Completing the probate search is the most likely path to advancing the conclusion to **Proved**.

Would you like me to execute the probate search (pli_006) by browsing the image-only collection on FamilySearch?

Now let me read the current project state.Good — I have a clear picture of the project. Let me map out what needs to change:

- **Active plan `pl_002`** has one remaining item: `pli_006` — probate, `1870-1890`, `in_progress`. Its date range rests on the assumption that Thomas Flynn died in that window.
- The new reference points to **~1865**, which shifts everything to the **1860-1870** period.
- This is a **Supersede** scenario: the active plan has unfinished items, but new information invalidates its core assumption. The old plan gets marked `superseded` (preserving the audit trail), and a fresh plan corrects the date window and broadens the search strategy to match the new target period.

Before writing, let me run a quick locality survey against the 1860–1870 window to confirm what's actually available.Good. The survey confirms:
- **Probate** — FamilySearch "Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994" (collection 1417683) — confirmed available, covers 1860–1870 ✓
- **Church records** — FamilySearch "Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985" (collection 1401638) — covers Catholic parishes including St. Mary's Pottsville (already referenced in the project via src_005) ✓
- External links are sparse for this window (Ancestry tax collection tops out at 1801), so Ancestry probate is the best external fallback

Now executing the supersede + new plan write in one batched call — the update to `pl_002` goes first so the validator sees only one active plan for `q_001`.

Predicted IDs: `pl_003` (new plan); `pli_007` through `pli_012` (six items, continuing from existing `pli_006`).The batched `ops` call requires top-level `section` and `op` to satisfy the schema even when using the ops array. Retrying with those set:All writes succeeded. Here's what changed and what the new plan looks like:

---

## Plan revision complete

**Research question:** q_001 — Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, PA, d. 1908)?

### What changed
- `pl_002` → **superseded** (its `pli_006` probate item with the `1870-1890` date range is now part of the audit trail, not the active work queue)
- `pl_003` → **new active plan**, 6 items, all targeting the revised **1860-1870** window

---

### New plan — pl_003

| # | ID | Record type | Date range | Repository | Purpose |
|---|-----|-------------|------------|------------|---------|
| 1 | `pli_007` | Census | 1870 | FamilySearch | Check for Thomas's **absence** from the 1870 census — a widow Flynn household would bracket his death before 1870 and may list surviving children |
| 2 | `pli_008` | Probate | 1860–1870 | FamilySearch | Thomas Flynn will/administration in Schuylkill County probate records (collection 1417683) — a will naming Patrick is direct parentage evidence; **core item** |
| 3 | `pli_009` | Church | 1860–1870 | FamilySearch | St. Mary's Catholic Church, Pottsville — burial register for Thomas ~1865 (same parish that baptized Patrick in 1845; collection 1401638) |
| 4 | `pli_010` | Vital record | 1860–1870 | FamilySearch | Pennsylvania death registration (1852+, poor rural compliance before 1906) — long-shot but free to check |
| 5 | `pli_011` | Tax | 1860–1870 | FamilySearch | County tax rolls — Thomas's last year assessed + first year absent brackets his death window precisely |
| 6 | `pli_012` | Probate | 1860–1870 | Ancestry | Fallback for `pli_008` — independent Ancestry indexing if FamilySearch probate yields nothing |

**Fallback link:** if step 2 (FamilySearch probate) is negative → step 6 (Ancestry probate).

**Estimated scope:** 6 searches.

---

### Why this ordering works
Steps 1 and 2 are the highest-yield items — the 1870 census quickly confirms whether Thomas was dead by then, and the probate records (confirmed available back to 1683) are the most direct path to parentage evidence. Church records (step 3) leverage the known connection to St. Mary's Pottsville. The tax roll (step 5) provides the tightest possible death-date bracket if probate and church records come up empty.

**Ready to start searching?** Step 1 (1870 census on FamilySearch) is the logical first move — say the word and I'll hand off to `search-records`.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identified the supersede scenario, marked pl_002 as superseded (preserving the audit trail), and created a new active plan pl_003 with six items targeting the revised 1860-1870 window. The new rationale (pli_008) explicitly references the superseded plan and explains why the prior 1870-1890 date range was invalidated. All claims align with the input state and MCP responses.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed all requirements: superseded the old plan without modifying its items, created a new active plan with 1860-1870 date ranges, explained why prior assumptions were invalidated, included FAN items (census, probate, church, vital, tax), and maintained free-before-paid sequencing with explicit fallback linking (pli_012 fallback_for pli_008).

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All three MCP calls used correct arguments. collections_search and external_links_search both passed standardPlace matching the Schuylkill jurisdiction with 1860-1870 date range. research_append correctly targeted /plans and /plan_items sections with proper schema (update pl_002 status first, then append pl_003 and six plan items). No fixture_not_found errors; matched.kind values are either 'predicate' (correct matches) or 'live' (write operation).

dimensions.name: Record type selection
dimensions.rationale: Every plan item's record_type matches clear information needs: census (1870 to confirm death before that year), probate (core parentage evidence via will naming Patrick), church burial (corroborate ~1865 death date at known parish), vital record (death registration if available), tax (bracket death year precisely), probate fallback (independent indexing). Rationales on each item explain what specific information the record type provides.

dimensions.name: Sequencing logic
dimensions.rationale: Sequence reflects defensible search order: free indexed (census, vital, tax) before paid external (Ancestry); highest-yield items first (census, probate); fallback chain explicit via pli_012 fallback_for pli_008. Rationales explain placement: census as fastest bracket of death window, probate as most direct parentage evidence, church records leveraging known connection, tax rolls providing precision if others fail.

dimensions.name: Jurisdiction accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Jurisdictions correctly reflect the 1860-1870 period: Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania was the appropriate county for both Thomas Flynn's probate/tax records and the location of St. Mary's Catholic Church, Pottsville. No historical boundary changes affect this period for this county. Modern Pennsylvania jurisdiction correctly applied to the antebellum timeframe.

dimensions.name: Plan mode and lifecycle
dimensions.rationale: Correctly chose supersede mode: new evidence (Thomas died ~1865) invalidated pl_002's core assumption (death in 1870-1890 window). pl_002 marked superseded, preserving its items as audit trail; pl_003 created as new active plan for q_001. Exactly one plan is active (pl_003); no duplicate active plans. Lifecycle properly maintained: prior plan's status changed before new plan activated.
